title.en: React vs Vue: Which Is Better for Screenshot to Code?

h1.en: React vs Vue for Screenshot to Code

description.en: A practical comparison of React JSX and Vue SFC output from AI screenshot-to-code tools — covering syntax differences, component structure, styling approaches, and which to choose for your project.

intro.en: When using AI screenshot-to-code tools, both React and Vue are excellent targets — but the generated code looks very different. React output is a .jsx (or .tsx) file using JSX syntax; Vue output is a .vue Single File Component with separate template, script, and style sections. This guide helps you understand the differences and choose the right framework output for your workflow.

content.en: The most visible difference is syntax. React JSX embeds HTML-like markup directly in JavaScript, using className instead of class and camelCase event handlers like onClick. Vue templates use HTML-like syntax with directives: v-if for conditionals, v-for for loops, :class for dynamic class binding, @click for event handling. For developers unfamiliar with both, Vue templates feel more like HTML while React JSX feels more like JavaScript. AI-generated code for both is readable, but Vue output tends to be cleaner for developers with an HTML background, while React output feels more natural for JavaScript-first developers.

A React component is a single .jsx file containing a function that returns JSX, with styles either inline, in a separate .css file, or as Tailwind classes. A Vue Single File Component (.vue) is a single file with three explicit sections: <template> for markup, <script setup> for Composition API logic, and <style scoped> for component-specific CSS. Vue's SFC structure enforces clear separation of concerns within a single file. React's flexibility means styling can be handled many different ways. When AI generates both, the Vue output tends to be more self-contained; React output integrates better with ecosystem-standard tools like styled-components or CSS Modules.

React uses explicit hooks: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). Vue uses reactive refs: const count = ref(0), accessed in templates as {{ count }}. Both patterns are well-understood by their respective ecosystems. AI-generated React code correctly uses useState for local state and useEffect for side effects. AI-generated Vue code uses ref, computed, and watch from the Composition API. For screenshots with interactive elements (toggles, counters, expandable sections), both frameworks' generated code includes the right reactive scaffolding.

React has a larger global ecosystem and is the dominant framework for enterprise frontend development in 2026. If you're building with Next.js, Remix, or working in a React shop, React output integrates immediately. Vue has a particularly strong following in the Asia-Pacific region, Laravel PHP projects (via Inertia.js), and teams that prefer a more opinionated framework. Vue also powers Nuxt.js, the Next.js equivalent for the Vue ecosystem. Generated Vue components drop directly into Nuxt pages; generated React components drop directly into Next.js apps.

In practice, the performance of AI-generated React and Vue components is equivalent for typical UI components — both are fast and the rendering difference is negligible. Where performance matters, both frameworks require optimization techniques (memoization in React with useMemo/useCallback, computed properties in Vue). AI-generated code does not automatically include these optimizations; they should be added manually during code review for performance-critical components.

question.en: Is React or Vue output from AI more accurate?

answer.en: Both achieve similar visual accuracy from the same screenshot. The main difference is in framework conventions applied — React output uses JSX and hooks patterns, Vue output uses template directives and Composition API. Neither is more visually accurate than the other; choose based on your project's tech stack.

question.en: Can I convert React output to Vue or vice versa?

answer.en: Manual conversion is straightforward for experienced developers since both represent the same component structure. class becomes className in React, @click becomes onClick, v-if becomes a ternary in JSX. For large-scale conversions, generate fresh from the screenshot in the target framework rather than converting generated code.

We recommend whichever framework your project already uses. If you're starting a new project, React with Next.js or Vue with Nuxt.js are both excellent choices in 2026. React has a broader job market and larger ecosystem; Vue has better documentation for beginners and a more gentle learning curve.

question.en: Does Vue output work in a Laravel + Inertia.js project?

answer.en: Yes. Generated Vue 3 SFCs work directly in Inertia.js applications. Copy the .vue file into your resources/js/Pages or resources/js/Components directory, and Inertia handles the integration with your Laravel backend. No modifications are needed to the generated component code.
